jokes.text: I never should have done Lindsay first. Lindsay's having a widening spurt.
jokes.setup: Father applying suntan lotion to family members
jokes.punchline: Inadvertent sexual innuendo about daughter's body changes
jokes.explanation: Classic AD inappropriate father comment with double entendre. Economy is strong - maximum awkwardness per word.

jokes.text: I think Buster was actually talking about Mom.
jokes.setup: George Sr. misunderstood who Buster was referencing regarding 'doing fine in the lady department'
jokes.punchline: Michael clarifies Buster meant their mother, making George Sr.'s response even more inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe escalation. The realization hits both George Sr. and the audience simultaneously. Economy is exceptional - one line completely recontextualizes the previous exchange.

jokes.text: Oh, yes. No, uh... I thought... you were talking about recent... Yes, thank you. Yes, she is, uh... She is quite the beauty.
jokes.setup: George Sr. realizes he was praising his wife's attractiveness to his son
jokes.punchline: His stammering, awkward attempt to recover from the misunderstanding
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to the previous cringe moment, but lacks the punch of the setup. The stammering works for character but reduces comedic efficiency.

jokes.text: Well, that's the downside of having a mother who never ages.
jokes.setup: Kids complaining they can't win the family beauty pageant
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s backhanded compliment about Lucille's appearance
jokes.explanation: Sharp character work - George Sr.'s twisted pride in his wife mixed with casual cruelty to his children. Good quotable structure.

jokes.text: It must be something in these cigarettes.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Lucille's ageless appearance
jokes.punchline: She credits cigarettes for keeping her young
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lucille moment - absurd anti-health logic delivered with complete confidence. Subverts every anti-smoking message while staying true to character.

jokes.text: I'm sure you'll all be thin by Thursday, Lindsay.
jokes.setup: Father addressing his children about the family pageant
jokes.punchline: Casually cruel comment about Lindsay's weight in front of everyone
jokes.explanation: Classic Bluth family cruelty. The casual delivery makes it funnier than an obvious fat joke. George Sr.'s complete lack of awareness is key.

jokes.text: It's not a dog collar. It's Buster's Gentleman Collar.
jokes.setup: Someone questions why Buster wears a dog collar
jokes.punchline: Lucille's euphemistic renaming doesn't change what it obviously is
jokes.explanation: Perfect example of Lucille's denial and class pretensions. The euphemism makes it funnier, not less obvious. Great character-specific wordplay.

jokes.text: Yeah, I barely know it's... Well, he's grown a little.
jokes.setup: Lucille claiming Buster barely notices his collar
jokes.punchline: Buster starts to agree but the collar is clearly too tight
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy setup. The interrupted sentence and Lucille's matter-of-fact response work well together. Visual element enhances the joke.

jokes.text: You bought me a dorky, lame, stupid, gay magic kit?
jokes.setup: Michael sees Gob has bought what appears to be a gift
jokes.punchline: Michael's harsh judgment followed by Gob revealing it's for himself
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection setup, though the payoff is somewhat predictable given Gob's character. The list of adjectives has good rhythm.

jokes.text: So I named it after her: Lucille Ball.
jokes.setup: Buster talks about a friend his mother made for him
jokes.punchline: It's named after both his mother and the I Love Lucy star, but it's clearly a ball
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that works on multiple levels. Shows Buster's childlike nature while creating a pun that references classic TV. Gains on rewatch.

jokes.text: Take that back! You take that back! We're normal! We're normal!
jokes.setup: Someone suggests Lucille is ruining the family
jokes.punchline: Buster's violent overreaction while wearing a dog collar proves the opposite
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic moment - Buster's desperate assertion of normalcy while demonstrating the exact opposite. The physical context makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: Okay. Down, down. Easy, Buster. Easy, boy. Sit.
jokes.setup: Buster is having an outburst while wearing his collar
jokes.punchline: Family members treat him exactly like a dog with commands
jokes.explanation: Logical extension of the dog collar joke. The family's casual treatment of Buster as a pet is both disturbing and funny.

jokes.text: I've never even got my suit wet from the outside.
jokes.setup: Buster expressing desire to go swimming
jokes.punchline: His bizarre way of describing never having been in water
jokes.explanation: Uniquely Buster phrasing that reveals both his sheltered life and odd way of thinking. 'From the outside' is a perfectly absurd qualifier.

jokes.text: Back to whence it came.
jokes.setup: Gob making a dramatic prediction about Michael's bird dying
jokes.punchline: He uses archaic language pretentiously, then repeats it for emphasis
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Gob's pretentious attempts at gravitas. The repetition suggests he's proud of the phrasing.

jokes.text: This is what happens when you're rich and white in America.
jokes.setup: The family is at a police station
jokes.punchline: Lucille's completely backwards understanding of privilege and persecution
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lucille moment - complete lack of self-awareness about privilege delivered with absolute conviction. Highly quotable social satire.

jokes.text: but it's nice he's giving the help the night off.
jokes.setup: Discussing how Buster is seen as the killer
jokes.punchline: George Sr. assumes Buster would give servants time off before committing murder
jokes.explanation: Reveals the family's warped priorities and class obsessions even in crisis. The casual assumption about having 'help' is perfectly tone-deaf.

jokes.text: Tomorrow... No, I guess it'll stop today. Today's the last day.
jokes.setup: George Sr. saying they won't stop until one of them is a murderer
jokes.punchline: His realization that if it's murder, there's only one crime
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan moment showing George Sr. working through the logic. The pause and correction create good timing.

jokes.text: And really, we're just asking this Asian gentleman and his wife...? To move over to that group of chairs there, freeing up the end seat, which I would love to take a shot at sitting in.
jokes.setup: George Sr. talking about family unity in crisis
jokes.punchline: He's actually just trying to get strangers to move so he can have a better seat
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection - what seemed like genuine concern for family is revealed as petty seating preference. The awkward racial identification adds cringe.

jokes.text: I can't spend any time with anybody under 25. About Buster.
jokes.setup: Tobias explaining his legal restrictions
jokes.punchline: The restriction ironically applies to his case subject
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about Tobias's legal troubles intersecting with family crisis. Economy is excellent - maximum irony in minimal words.

jokes.text: Son of a bitch!
jokes.setup: Evidence supervisor finds his parking spot taken by the stair car
jokes.punchline: His angry reaction before realizing it's evidence in a murder case
jokes.explanation: Good situational comedy - relatable parking anger before the ironic revelation. The narrator's explanation enhances the absurdity.

jokes.text: In fairness to him, it had taken him a super long time to get a decent parking spot that he didn't have to walk, like, a mile from.
jokes.setup: Evidence supervisor damaged crucial evidence in anger
jokes.punchline: Narrator's sympathetic justification for destroying evidence
jokes.explanation: Good narrator moment finding universal relatability in an absurd situation. The casual tone makes the excuse funnier.

jokes.text: Poker face.
jokes.setup: Michael tells Buster to put on his poker face for the cameras
jokes.punchline: Buster's attempt at a poker face makes him look more guilty
jokes.explanation: Visual comedy that works even in transcript form. The concept that Buster's neutral face looks criminal is perfectly character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Even though they do say, in most cases, something happens in childhood and, usually, the person that raises them is to blame. But that's experts.
jokes.setup: George Sr. saying it's not time to play the blame game
jokes.punchline: He immediately cites expert opinion that would blame Lucille
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - George Sr. deflecting blame while simultaneously assigning it. The 'but that's experts' disclaimer is perfectly dismissive.

jokes.text: Don't you try to pin this on Lupe. She had her own family to raise, too.
jokes.setup: Discussion of who raised Buster
jokes.punchline: Lucille defending the housekeeper who actually raised her son
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lucille deflection revealing her neglect while appearing protective of staff

jokes.text: Then I should be its ringmaster.
jokes.setup: Michael says dealing with media will be a circus
jokes.punchline: Tobias eagerly volunteers to be ringmaster
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tobias moment - taking a metaphor literally and seeing opportunity for performance. Shows his theatrical delusions.

jokes.text: As you see, it's latex, so it can go right up your ass.
jokes.setup: Someone demonstrating a product
jokes.punchline: Casual, inappropriate description of the product's capabilities
jokes.explanation: Shock humor with moderate effectiveness. Lacks the character integration and setup that would elevate it beyond crude surprise.

jokes.text: raised by a young mother who had him at 17, whose only flaw was that... she loved him... too much. Actually, I was 37.
jokes.setup: Tobias creating a sympathetic narrative about Lucille
jokes.punchline: Lucille immediately corrects the age lie
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with perfect character moments. Tobias's spin doctoring meets Lucille's vanity and honesty. The timing is excellent.

jokes.text: They're threatening our feet?
jokes.setup: Discussion of Chinese investors' threatening message
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's confused interpretation of 'get back on your feet'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lindsay moment - missing obvious metaphor for literal threat. Her confusion is believable and character-appropriate.

jokes.text: You don't think these Chinese would first try to, I don't know, ding your credit rating? It's their culture.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Chinese investor threats
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s casual racist stereotype about financial threats
jokes.explanation: Relies on stereotyping for humor, showing George Sr.'s casual racism but not particularly clever

jokes.text: Right. With him, you knew where you stood. Or... Or couldn't.
jokes.setup: George Sr.'s comment about knowing where you stood with Saddam
jokes.punchline: Lucille's dark pun about amputees not being able to stand
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark wordplay. Lucille builds on George Sr.'s comment with a brutal pun that's both clever and horrifying. Great economy.

jokes.text: This is why we never told you about the beach house, Michael. Uh, can I add something? This is why we never told you about the beach house.
jokes.setup: Michael suggests selling the beach house
jokes.punchline: Both parents immediately explain why they hid it, with same reason
jokes.explanation: Good running gag setup with perfect family dynamic. The redundancy and synchronized response shows their unified front against Michael.

jokes.text: I mean, Jesus Christ, $7,000 to find out that Ann Veal had nothing to do with Buster's case?
jokes.setup: The expensive lawyers' investigation
jokes.punchline: Michael's outrage at the cost of eliminating an obviously innocent suspect
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to the Ann Veal joke, emphasizing the absurd waste of money. Michael's frustration is relatable and justified.

jokes.text: A little.
jokes.setup: George Michael asking if his relationship is over
jokes.punchline: Rebel echoes his earlier 'you can't sell a company a little' with relationship context
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback structure. One word carries the weight of the entire previous argument about degrees of finality. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: A foot. They're threatening our feet? Is it a foot now? They shortened it.
jokes.setup: Earlier confusion about 'taking a foot' vs 'taking an inch'
jokes.punchline: The threat has apparently escalated and shortened simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Good callback that builds on earlier confusion. The logic is absurd but follows the family's pattern of misunderstanding.

jokes.text: Well, not 'chop, chop,' not... That's a bad choice of words. For Christ's sake.
jokes.setup: Michael telling George Michael to hurry up with the software
jokes.punchline: He realizes 'chop chop' is inappropriate given Chinese threats
jokes.explanation: Good self-aware moment as Michael catches his own poor word choice. Shows how the threats are affecting his thinking.

jokes.text: Exactly, and everyone knows the workday doesn't start until nine to five.
jokes.setup: Gob complaining about event timing
jokes.punchline: He gets the phrase 'nine to five' backwards
jokes.explanation: Good malapropism showing Gob's fundamental misunderstanding of work schedules. Reveals his privileged disconnect from normal employment.

jokes.text: future rapists.
jokes.setup: Michael giving speech about hardworking migrant hoping to feed his three young children
jokes.punchline: He accidentally reveals his prejudiced view
jokes.explanation: Darkly brilliant moment where Michael's attempt at progressive rhetoric breaks down. The pause before the reveal maximizes impact.

jokes.text: My name is Gob Bluth, and as president of Fakeblock, I have an announcement! I'm building a wall!
jokes.setup: Michael's carefully planned announcement
jokes.punchline: Gob crashes it with his own competing announcement
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gob moment of narcissistic scene-stealing. The competing announcements create maximum chaos and family dysfunction.

jokes.text: Yup. This is how it ended for the honeybee, as well.
jokes.setup: George Michael fires Michael
jokes.punchline: Narrator references Tobias's earlier bee parable about wall builders
jokes.explanation: Excellent long-form callback to obscure earlier reference. Shows AD's commitment to rewarding careful viewers with layered payoffs.

jokes.text: You're fired. Yup. This is how it ended for the honeybee, as well.
jokes.setup: George Michael firing his father
jokes.punchline: Narrator's callback to earlier honeybee parable reference
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback with perfect timing, connecting the firing to the earlier wall-builder metaphor

jokes.text: Guys, guys, guys, this fits with the fact that he can't walk. And has been carried everywhere he went, damn it. Which means what, G-Man? He couldn't have committed this crime. In any way.
jokes.setup: Lawyers analyzing evidence against Buster
jokes.punchline: They realize Buster's physical limitations make him innocent
jokes.explanation: Good logical conclusion to the legal investigation. The lawyers' expertise finally produces useful results, though it took expensive trial and error.
